raceDetails.courseSummary: The 2026 Australian Road National Championships moved to Perth, Western Australia for a three-year stint after two decades at Buninyong in Victoria. The 177km elite men's race covers 13 laps of a 13.6km circuit through Perth's CBD and the leafy Kings Park. Unlike Buninyong's defining single climb, Perth's course spreads the climbing over an uphill drag in the first half and punchy pinches in the final stages. The defining feature is Malcolm Street - dubbed 'Pedal Mafia Mountain' - a steep pinch that peaks just 400 metres before the finish line, making for an explosive finale on every lap.

keySectors.name: Malcolm Street (Pedal Mafia Mountain)
keySectors.description: The course's defining feature - a steep pinch climb peaking 400m before the finish line. Repeated 13 times, it becomes more decisive as the race progresses.

keyClimbs.notes: Uphill drag through leafy Kings Park - first half of each lap

keyClimbs.name: Malcolm Street

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Australian Road National Championships have been held annually since 1901, making it one of the oldest national championships in world cycling. The event spent two decades on a fixed course near Ballarat, Victoria, with its race-shaping Buninyong climb becoming iconic. The 2025 move to Perth marked a new era, with a circuit format that rewards different rider characteristics than the climbing-heavy Buninyong course.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for the Malcolm Street pinch on each lap - as fatigue builds through 13 laps, this explosive climb becomes increasingly selective. The Perth course proved more decisive than it appears on paper in 2025, with few opportunities to rest. The flat-ish final kilometers after Malcolm Street make it possible for a small group to stay away, but also allows sprinters to hang on if they survive the climb. Expect attacking racing from the Perth locals eager to perform in front of home fans.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour El Salvador is a 2.1 category stage race covering 351.1km over five stages (including a prologue) from January 17-21 across El Salvador. The route showcases the Central American nation's diverse terrain, from the historic capital San Salvador to the Pacific coast at Surf City, through volcanic highlands to the colonial city of Santa Ana. Over 120 cyclists from 12 countries compete in this growing international event that highlights El Salvador's scenic roads and passionate cycling culture.
narratives: El Salvador's growing prominence as a cycling destination, hosting this event for the third time
The race showcases diverse terrain from Pacific coast beaches to volcanic highlands
International field of 120+ riders from 12 countries competing on Central American roads
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Tour El Salvador has established itself as one of the premier cycling events in Central America, bringing international attention to El Salvador's roads and landscapes. The race passes through iconic locations including the Centro Historico of San Salvador, the surfing beaches of El Tunco (Surf City), and the western highlands around Santa Ana and Cerro Verde.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for the mountainous stages through the volcanic highlands where GC contenders will battle for time. The heat and humidity add an extra dimension to racing in Central America.

stages.name: Stage 1: Willunga to Willunga
stages.description: Opening stage starting and finishing in Willunga. Features two mid-stage ascents and a short uphill finish suited to punchy sprinters.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The Women's Tour Down Under opens in Willunga, the home of the iconic hill that has become synonymous with TDU. However, Stage 1 bypasses the famous climb, instead featuring two mid-stage ascents at 53.5km and 95.4km before a short uphill sprint finish. The 475m climb to the finish line (5.6% gradient, 20m elevation) favors punchy sprinters who can power up the modest incline.

keyClimbs.name: Willunga Circuit Climb
keyClimbs.notes: Short uphill sprint finish - punchy sprinters' territory

stageDetails.watchNotes: A stage for the powerful sprinters who can handle a short uphill finale. Watch for positioning battles on the circuit and attacks from riders who want to test their legs early. Two intermediate sprints at Snapper Point (17.2km and 114km) add bonus opportunities.

stages.name: Stage 2: Magill to Paracombe
stages.description: Queen stage featuring dual ascents of the iconic Corkscrew Road - the decisive stage for GC.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The queen stage of the Women's Tour Down Under features very little flat terrain. Starting from Magill's debut as a TDU host, the course rolls up and down through the Adelaide Hills before tackling two ascents of the punishing Corkscrew Road. The dual Corkscrew climb finale has been added for 2026, making this the decisive stage for the overall classification. Paracombe hosts the finish for the first time.

keyClimbs.name: Corkscrew Road (1st)
keyClimbs.notes: First ascent - sets up the finale

keyClimbs.name: Corkscrew Road (2nd)
keyClimbs.notes: Final climb - decisive moment for GC

stageDetails.watchNotes: The Corkscrew's 9.7% average gradient with pitches up to 14% will shatter the peloton. The dual ascent format is new for 2026, allowing GC contenders two shots at making the decisive move. Expect attacks on both climbs as riders fight for the ochre jersey. The rolling terrain throughout means no recovery time for tired legs.

stages.name: Stage 3: Norwood to Campbelltown
stages.description: Final stage with circuit finish in Campbelltown - last chance to shake up the GC.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The final stage takes riders from Norwood to a circuit finish in Campbelltown. While not as brutal as the Corkscrew stage, the hilly terrain still provides opportunities for late attacks. The circuit format adds unpredictability to the finale, giving aggressive riders multiple chances to test the GC leader.
stageDetails.watchNotes: The final battle for the ochre jersey. If the GC is tight, expect nervous racing and potential attacks. The circuit finish rewards riders who can read the race and time their final effort. Team tactics will be crucial in protecting or attacking the overall lead.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 10th edition of the Women's Santos Tour Down Under takes place over three stages from January 17-19 in South Australia. The race traverses the Adelaide Hills and surrounding wine regions, featuring a mix of hilly terrain and potential summit finishes. As the opening race of the women's WorldTour calendar, it sets the tone for the season and attracts the world's top women's teams to the Southern Hemisphere's premier stage race.

keyClimbs.name: Willunga Hill
keyClimbs.notes: Iconic climb of the Tour Down Under, steep and punchy with a demanding gradient

keyClimbs.name: Paracombe

The 10th anniversary edition of this growing women's stage race

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Women's Tour Down Under was first held in 2016 as a companion event to the long-established men's race. Based around Adelaide and its surrounding hills and wine country, the race has grown into a prestigious WorldTour event that launches the women's international road season each January.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The hilly stages through the Adelaide Hills will shape the GC. Watch for Willunga Hill and Paracombe as the decisive climbs where GC contenders show their early-season form.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Benidorm World Cup round features a unique Mediterranean cyclocross course along the Costa Blanca. The sandy, dry circuit is a departure from the typical muddy Belgian CX races, with technical sections weaving through parkland near the beach. The course includes sand sections, steep run-ups, and off-camber turns that reward bike handling skills. The warm January climate in southeastern Spain creates fast, dry conditions that favor riders comfortable on hard-packed terrain.

raceDetails.historicalContext: Benidorm joined the UCI Cyclocross World Cup calendar as a relatively new venue, bringing cyclocross to the Spanish Mediterranean coast. The venue provides a sharp contrast to the traditional Belgian and Dutch muddy courses, offering sandy and dry conditions that test different skill sets.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for the sand sections where gaps open up, and the technical off-camber turns where bike handling makes the difference. The dry conditions often allow for faster racing and more aggressive riding.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour Down Under opens the WorldTour season each January in South Australia. This 6-stage race (including prologue) features two decisive mountain stages in the Adelaide Hills - Stage 2 on the feared Corkscrew Road and Stage 4 featuring the iconic triple ascent of Willunga Hill. The format creates multiple GC opportunities: the prologue establishes early gaps, Corkscrew Road on Stage 2 provides the first major test, and Willunga Hill typically decides the ochre jersey on Stage 4. The final stage now features a circuit finish rather than a traditional sprint, adding drama to the finale.

keyClimbs.name: Willunga Hill
keyClimbs.notes: Triple ascent on queen stage - decisive for GC. 3km at 7.5% avg, final ascent to finish line.

keyClimbs.name: Corkscrew Road
keyClimbs.notes: Double ascent - early GC shaker. 2.5km at 9.7% avg, up to 16% max. First serious test of legs.

keyClimbs.name: Norton Summit
keyClimbs.notes: Opening climb on Stage 2, sets up Corkscrew attacks.

keyClimbs.name: Menglers Hill
keyClimbs.notes: Triple ascent in Barossa Valley, opener for punchy riders.

Three Willunga ascents unprecedented - the queen stage will rewrite TDU history
New circuit finish at Stirling creates unpredictable finale - can GC leaders survive?
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Tour Down Under has been the traditional WorldTour season opener since 1999, held annually in the Adelaide region of South Australia. Willunga Hill became a TDU staple in 2008 and has since become one of the most iconic climbs in Australian cycling, consistently producing exciting racing and GC drama. Past champions include a who's who of Grand Tour winners and classics specialists.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The race is won and lost in the Adelaide Hills. Focus on Corkscrew Road (Stage 2) for early GC moves, then Willunga Hill (Stage 4) for the decisive battle. The prologue gaps can prove crucial - even 10-15 seconds provides valuable buffer for mountain stages. Watch for heat-related tactics and the new circuit finale format on Stage 5.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: Prologue creates initial gaps → Stage 2 Corkscrew provides first serious selection → Stage 3 transitional day with breakaway potential → Stage 4 Willunga typically decides GC → Stage 5 circuit finale can produce late attacks

stages.name: Prologue: Adelaide → Adelaide (ITT)
stages.description: Short 3.6km individual time trial from the City of Adelaide Tour Village to Victoria Park. Flat run-out with a twisting conclusion - a sharp wake-up call for legs after the off-season.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A 3.6km twilight time trial through Adelaide's heart - only the second prologue in TDU history. Starting from the City of Adelaide Tour Village in the CBD, riders face a straight, flat run-out before a twisting technical conclusion in Victoria Park. Race Director Stuart O'Grady calls it 'a violent effort and stark wake-up call for riders' legs after months away from competition.'
stageDetails.watchNotes: The prologue shakes up race dynamics from day one - riders 10-20 seconds behind will need to attack later in the week. TT specialists and GC contenders will be fighting for every second. Watch for the technical corners in Victoria Park where time can be gained or lost.

stages.name: Stage 1: Tanunda → Tanunda
stages.description: Circuit through Barossa wine country with three ascents of Menglers Hill. Flat finish after a fast descent suits the sprinters, but breakaways could survive.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The first road stage circles through the stunning Barossa wine country, starting and finishing in Tanunda. Three ascents of Menglers Hill provide the climbing challenge, but this year's direction makes the climb gentler than previous editions. A wickedly fast descent leads to a flat run-in of 13km to the finish.

keyClimbs.name: Menglers Hill
keyClimbs.notes: Climbed 3 times - gentler direction than 2025

stageDetails.watchNotes: Sprinters' teams will control the peloton on the flat finish, but breakaways have a chance if they survive the Menglers Hill circuits. Watch for white-knuckle moments on the fast descent before the final 13km. Last year Sam Welsford won the sprint here despite a late crash.

stages.name: Stage 2: Norwood → Uraidla
stages.description: The feared Corkscrew Road (9.7% avg) features twice - midway and 14km from the finish. Also includes Norton Summit and Mount Lofty climbs. A true GC shaker.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The queen stage of the Adelaide Hills - a true GC shaker with two ascents of the feared Corkscrew Road. Starting in Norwood, riders tempo through Norton Summit and tackle Mount Lofty before the first Corkscrew assault at mid-stage. The second Corkscrew comes with 14km to go, followed by an undulating approach to Uraidla. A downhill run-in offers one last chance for the peloton to catch escapees.

keyClimbs.name: Corkscrew Road (1st)
keyClimbs.notes: First of two ascents

keyClimbs.name: Corkscrew Road (2nd)
keyClimbs.notes: Final climb - decisive moment

stageDetails.watchNotes: The Corkscrew's 9.7% average gradient is brutal - expect attacks on both ascents. The undulating 14km after the final Corkscrew will test any solo riders' wills. GC contenders will show their cards here. This is where the race for the ochre jersey truly begins.

stages.name: Stage 3: Henley Beach → Nairne
stages.description: Starting at the beach but no holiday - Wickham Hill (3km at 7%, max 11%) comes early. Mount Barker climb at 115km is short but tough, possibly not enough to drop sprinters.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Starting at idyllic seaside suburb Henley Beach (hosting a stage start for the first time), this is no holiday - punchy climbs await. Wickham Hill comes early at 32km, followed by Chandlers Hill. Mount Barker Summit with less than 5km to the finish provides the final test. First-ever stage finish in the Mount Barker Council town of Nairne.

keyClimbs.name: Wickham Hill
keyClimbs.notes: 207m elevation gain - too early for GC attacks

keyClimbs.name: Mount Barker Summit
keyClimbs.notes: Short but tough - may not drop sprinters

stageDetails.watchNotes: This is the Adelaide Epic Ride route for public participants. Wickham Hill's 11% max gradient is punishing early on. The Mount Barker climb is short but could still cause damage on tired legs. Sprinters may survive if their teams control the pace - watch for a potential bunch sprint in Nairne.

stages.name: Stage 4: Brighton → Willunga Hill
stages.description: Queen stage with 2,500m+ climbing. Three ascents of iconic Willunga Hill including two in the final 26km. The longest and hardest day - a climber's paradise.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The longest and hardest day - the iconic queen stage with over 2,500m of climbing. Starting in Brighton, Chandlers Hill comes in the first 15km. Then three ascents of the legendary Willunga Hill: first topping out after 50km, then two more in the final 26km via McLaren Flat and McLaren Vale. A climber's paradise with a summit finish on Australia's most famous cycling climb.

keyClimbs.name: Chandlers Hill
keyClimbs.notes: Early warm-up for legs

keyClimbs.name: Willunga Hill (1st)
keyClimbs.notes: First ascent after 50km

keyClimbs.name: Willunga Hill (2nd)
keyClimbs.notes: Second ascent - attacks begin

keyClimbs.name: Willunga Hill (Final)
keyClimbs.notes: Summit finish - decisive for GC

stageDetails.watchNotes: Willunga Hill is TDU's most iconic climb - expect huge crowds lining the road. The triple ascent in 2026 is a new twist on the classic. GC contenders must attack here or lose the race. Race Director O'Grady: 'It's certainly going to be a day for the climbers and the winner will really have to earn it.'

stages.name: Stage 5: Stirling → Stirling
stages.description: Eight laps of a punchy 21km circuit with saw-blade profile. Four classified climbs plus Mount Barker Road finish (2km, 4% avg but 11% max). World championship-style finale where anything can happen.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A revolutionary finale for the Tour Down Under - the final day moves from a sprint-friendly city stage to an aggressive world championship-style circuit. Eight laps of a punchy 21km circuit around Stirling with a saw-blade profile. Four classified climbs per lap before the finishing ascent on Mount Barker Road. Race Director O'Grady calls it 'the best final stage we've ever had.'

keyClimbs.name: Mount Barker Road (per lap)
keyClimbs.notes: 78m elevation gain - deceptive average hides 11% pitches

stageDetails.watchNotes: This is a whole new ball game for TDU. O'Grady: 'Even if you're within a minute of the lead, if the racing is hard and smart enough, you have the potential to destroy the ochre jersey.' Eight laps means repeated attacks and no hiding. Watch for aggressive racing on the punchy climbs and the decisive 11% gradients of Mount Barker Road. Stirling offers a perfect viewing platform with great food, drink options and shade.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Women's One-Day Race is a fast 94.2km criterium-style event held in Tanunda, the heart of South Australia's Barossa Valley wine country. Racing takes place on a 8km circuit, with riders completing approximately 12 laps through the historic town streets. The flat to rolling terrain with six intermediate sprints favors fast finishers and sprinters who can handle the repetitive circuit racing.

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Women's One-Day Race in Tanunda was added to the Santos Tour Down Under festival to give riders an additional competitive opportunity during the Australian summer racing block. The fast circuit racing provides a different test to the climbing-focused Women's Tour Down Under stages.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for breakaway attempts on the early laps as teams test each other. The six intermediate sprints keep the race animated throughout. In the closing laps, expect positioning battles as sprinters' teams try to control the front for a bunch kick. The flat circuit rewards tactical racing and good pack positioning.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Classica Camp de Morvedre is a 163.4km one-day race in the Valencia region of Spain. The route passes through the Camp de Morvedre comarca north of Valencia city, featuring the climb of Garbi as a key selection point. This race marks one of the first European road races of the season and has been upgraded from a 1.2 to 1.1 category, attracting stronger fields. The hilly terrain around the Valencian coast creates a selective course suited to puncheurs and aggressive riders.

keyClimbs.name: Garbi
keyClimbs.notes: The best-known climb in the region, with steep ramps that create selection in the peloton

narratives: One of the first European road races of the 2026 season
The upgrade to 1.1 category has brought stronger fields to this Valencian classic
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Classica Camp de Morvedre takes its name from the historical county in northern Valencia. The race is part of the growing Spanish early-season calendar that provides vital race days for teams preparing for the spring classics.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The climb of Garbi is where the race comes alive. Watch for attacks on the steep ramps that can break up the bunch before the finish.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Opening race of the Challenge Mallorca series, running from Marratxi to Felanitx in southeastern Mallorca. The route crosses the central plain of the island before finishing with a hilly terrain around Felanitx. Part of the Challenge Mallorca, a series of independent one-day races held over consecutive days that serve as the traditional European season opener on the Balearic island.
narratives: Season opener on Mallorca with WorldTour teams testing early-season form
Challenge Mallorca allows teams to rotate lineups across the different trofeos
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Challenge Mallorca (also known as Volta Ciclista a Mallorca) has been a fixture on the early-season calendar since the 1990s. The series of one-day races provides varied terrain across the island, from flat sprinter stages to mountain summit finishes in the Serra de Tramuntana.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Early-season form indicator for WorldTour teams beginning their European campaigns.

races.name: UCI Cyclocross World Cup Maasmechelen - Men Elite
races.description: The Maasmechelen round returns with its challenging course at the mining museum, featuring dramatic elevation changes and technical sections.

primary.notes: Full race replay available

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Maasmechelen World Cup round takes place in the former coal mining region of Belgian Limburg, at the Terhills site. The course uses the dramatic terrain of old mining slag heaps to create one of the most physically demanding courses on the World Cup calendar. Riders face steep climbs up loose, gravelly surfaces, technical descents, and sections through woodland. The January timing often brings cold, wet conditions that turn the already challenging terrain into a true test of power and bike handling.

keySectors.name: Slag Heap Climb
keySectors.description: Steep ascent up the former mining slag heap with loose gravel surface - the signature feature of this course

keySectors.name: Woodland Section
keySectors.description: Technical singletrack through the trees with roots and off-camber sections

narratives: Maasmechelen's mining heritage course is one of the toughest on the calendar
The penultimate World Cup round before the season finale means overall standings are still at stake
The steep slag heap climb is a decisive selection point where the strongest riders assert dominance
raceDetails.historicalContext: Maasmechelen's Terhills site, a former coal mining area transformed into a recreational zone, has become a regular fixture on the World Cup cyclocross calendar. The unique terrain of the old slag heaps provides elevation changes rarely seen in the flat Belgian landscape, creating a course that favors pure power climbers.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The slag heap climb is the race's decisive feature - watch for attacks on the steep, loose gravel ascent. The course's physical demands often string out the field early, making this a war of attrition from the first lap.

races.name: UCI Cyclocross World Cup Maasmechelen - Women Elite
races.description: The Maasmechelen round returns with its challenging course at the mining museum, featuring dramatic elevation changes and technical sections.

primary.notes: Full race replay available

raceDetails.courseSummary: The women's elite race at Maasmechelen uses the same demanding Terhills course built on former coal mining slag heaps in Belgian Limburg. The steep climbs on loose gravel, technical woodland sections, and challenging off-camber terrain create a selective course that rewards both power and technical skill. Women race fewer laps but face the same punishing terrain features including the signature slag heap climb.

keySectors.name: Slag Heap Climb
keySectors.description: Steep ascent up the former mining slag heap with loose gravel surface

keySectors.name: Woodland Section
keySectors.description: Technical singletrack through woodland with roots and off-camber turns

narratives: The rivalry between Fem van Empel and Puck Pieterse has defined the women's CX season
Lucinda Brand's consistency in World Cup rounds makes her a threat on any course
The physically demanding Maasmechelen course tends to favor pure power riders over technical specialists
raceDetails.historicalContext: The women's race at Maasmechelen has grown in stature alongside the venue's increasing prominence on the World Cup calendar. The demanding terrain rewards the same attributes as the men's race - raw power on the climbs and technical skill on the descents.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The slag heap climb often creates early separation in the women's race. Watch for which riders can maintain momentum on the loose gravel surface - the ability to ride (rather than run) the steepest sections can be decisive.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Clasica Comunitat Valenciana is a prestigious 1.1 one-day race that traditionally kicks off the European road cycling season proper. Based in the Valencia region of eastern Spain, the flat-to-rolling route typically favors fast finishers and sprinters. The race attracts strong WorldTour teams testing their early-season sprint trains and serves as an important form indicator for the spring campaigns ahead.
narratives: Traditionally one of the first major European road races of the season
WorldTour sprinter teams use this as an early test of their leadout formations
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Clasica Comunitat Valenciana occupies a distinguished position within the professional cycling calendar, serving as an early-season litmus test for the world's elite riders as they transition from winter training blocks into competitive racing. The race has grown in stature over recent years.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for the sprint teams' first test of their leadout formations. The flat terrain should produce a bunch sprint, but early-season unpredictability can create surprises.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Trofeo Palma Feminina is part of the Challenge Mallorca Femenina, a three-day women's racing series on the island. The route around Palma features hilly terrain including the Coll de Sa Creu climb near the capital, which provides a decisive selection point. The second edition of this women's series (first held in 2024) offers UCI 1.1 status, attracting top women's teams to the Mediterranean island for early-season racing.

keyClimbs.name: Coll de Sa Creu
keyClimbs.notes: Late climb near Palma that provides a launching pad for attacks in the final kilometers

narratives: The Challenge Mallorca Femenina is growing in prestige as a women's early-season series
The hilly terrain around Palma rewards aggressive racing from strong climbers and puncheurs
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Challenge Mallorca Femenina was introduced in 2024, adding a women's companion series to the long-established men's Challenge Mallorca. The series offers three consecutive days of UCI 1.1 racing, providing women's teams with competitive early-season racing on the Mediterranean island.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Coll de Sa Creu is the key selection point. Watch for attacks on this climb that can create a small lead group contesting the finish.

races.name: UCI Cyclocross World Cup Hoogerheide - Men Elite
races.description: The final World Cup round before Worlds, Hoogerheide serves as a crucial form test on its power-demanding Dutch circuit.

primary.notes: Full race replay available

raceDetails.courseSummary: Hoogerheide's GP Adrie van der Poel course is a power circuit with short punchy climbs, perfect for testing Worlds form.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Final form test before Worlds - watch who is peaking at the right time.

races.name: UCI Cyclocross World Cup Hoogerheide - Women Elite
races.description: The final World Cup round before Worlds, Hoogerheide serves as a crucial form test on its power-demanding Dutch circuit.

primary.notes: Full race replay available

raceDetails.courseSummary: Hoogerheide hosts the traditional World Cup season finale in the southern Netherlands, near the Belgian border. The course, designed by former professional cyclist Adrie van der Poel, winds through the grounds of a recreational park featuring grass sections, sand pits, steep run-ups, and technical descents. In January conditions, the grass becomes heavy mud that tests riders' stamina and bike handling. Hoogerheide has also frequently hosted the UCI Cyclocross World Championships, making it one of the sport's most storied venues.

keySectors.name: Sand Pit
keySectors.description: Deep sand section that often forces dismounts and can cause significant time gaps

keySectors.name: Steep Run-Up
keySectors.description: Signature steep climb requiring shouldering - one of the most iconic run-ups in CX

keySectors.name: Off-Camber Section
keySectors.description: Slippery off-camber grass turns that become treacherous in wet conditions

narratives: The season finale with World Cup overall points on the line
Hoogerheide often serves as a dress rehearsal for the World Championships
The Van der Poel-designed course at Hoogerheide is one of the most complete tests in cyclocross
raceDetails.historicalContext: Hoogerheide has been a cornerstone of the cyclocross calendar for decades. The course was designed by Adrie van der Poel, father of Mathieu and David van der Poel, and has hosted both World Cup rounds and World Championships. The GP Adrie van der Poel has been held here since 1986, making it one of the longest-running cyclocross events.
raceDetails.watchNotes: As the season finale, riders often leave everything on the course. The sand pit and steep run-up are the decisive features. Watch for the overall World Cup standings battle - riders may race tactically for points rather than just the win.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Second race of the Challenge Mallorca series, from Binissalem in the central wine-producing region to Andratx on the southwestern coast. The route heads west through the foothills of the Serra de Tramuntana mountain range, featuring rolling terrain and a hilly finish around Andratx. The varied profile typically suits puncheurs and aggressive riders.
narratives: Teams rotating lineups from the opening day to target specific trofeos matching their riders' strengths
raceDetails.historicalContext: Part of the Challenge Mallorca series that has run since the 1990s, the Trofeo Binissalem-Andratx takes riders through the western part of the island toward the scenic Andratx coast.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The rolling terrain heading into Andratx favors aggressive racing. Watch for attacks on the hillier sections near the finish.

races.name: AlUla Tour
races.description: 5-stage UCI ProSeries race through the ancient landscapes of AlUla, Saudi Arabia. Features sprint stages at the Camel Cup Track and challenging terrain culminating at Skyviews of Harrat Uwayrid.

broadcast.notes: Also available: L'Équipe (France), SBS (Australia), SSC (Middle East)

stages.name: Stage 1: AlUla Camel Cup Track → AlUla Camel Cup Track
stages.description: Opening circuit stage at the iconic Camelodrome. Sprint finish expected, but desert winds could create chaos.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Circuit stage starting and finishing at the AlUla Camel Cup Track. Flat terrain suited for sprinters, though crosswinds in the open desert could split the peloton.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Watch for echelons if winds pick up. Sprinter teams will control but conditions can be unpredictable.

stages.name: Stage 2: AlManshiyah Train Station → AlManshiyah Train Station
stages.description: Second sprint opportunity on another circuit stage through the desert landscape.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Circuit stage at the historic AlManshiyah Train Station. Another day for the fast men, with the possibility of crosswind action.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Sprinters day, but watch for aggressive GC riders if winds create opportunities.

stages.name: Stage 3: Winter Park → Bir Jaydah Mountain Wirkah
stages.description: First GC test with a mountain finish at Bir Jaydah. The terrain gets serious.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Point-to-point stage ending with the climb to Bir Jaydah Mountain. First real test for the GC contenders.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Key GC stage - expect attacks on the final climb.

stages.name: Stage 4: Winter Park → Hegra
stages.description: Queen stage with the longest distance. Finish at the UNESCO World Heritage site of Hegra.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The longest and most demanding stage, finishing at the ancient Nabataean city of Hegra (Mada'in Saleh). Rolling terrain throughout.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Attrition stage - the distance and accumulated fatigue will take their toll.

stages.name: Stage 5: AlUla Old Town → Skyviews of Harrat Uwayrid
stages.description: Final stage with summit finish at Skyviews of Harrat Uwayrid. Last chance to shake up the GC.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Final GC showdown with a summit finish at the dramatic Skyviews of Harrat Uwayrid viewpoint overlooking the volcanic landscape.
stageDetails.watchNotes: Summit finish decides the overall - expect fireworks on the final climb.

raceDetails.courseSummary: 5-stage race through Saudi Arabia's stunning AlUla region, featuring ancient archaeological sites and dramatic desert landscapes. Two sprint stages followed by three days in the hills, culminating with a summit finish.

gcContenders: Climbers and punchy GC riders will target the final three stages
sprinters: Fast men have two clear opportunities on stages 1-2
stageHunters: Breakaway specialists can target the hilly stages

narratives: First race of the UCI ProSeries calendar
WorldTour teams use this as early-season preparation
Desert conditions and potential crosswinds add unpredictability
raceDetails.historicalContext: The AlUla Tour began in 2020 as part of Saudi Arabia's Vision 2030 plan to develop sports tourism. The race showcases the UNESCO World Heritage site of Hegra and the stunning rock formations of AlUla.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Early-season form indicator for WorldTour teams. Watch for crosswinds on flat stages and GC action on the mountain finishes.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Trofeo Calvia starts and finishes in the municipality of Calvia on Mallorca's southwestern coast. The route features a mix of coastal and inland roads with rolling terrain. Calvia's varied topography including short punchy climbs makes this a race that can go to either sprinters or puncheurs depending on race dynamics.
narratives: Mid-week race in the Challenge Mallorca series with teams adapting strategies based on earlier results
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Trofeo Calvia is one of the core races in the Challenge Mallorca series. The Calvia municipality, which includes the resort areas of Magaluf and Santa Ponsa, provides a scenic coastal backdrop for early-season racing.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The terrain profile determines whether this is a sprinter's race or an opportunity for puncheurs. Watch for the final kilometers to see if any hills soften the sprint.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Women's Surf Coast Classic is a challenging 118km race from Lorne to Torquay along Victoria's spectacular Great Ocean Road coastline. The race features rolling terrain with a significant 10km climb at the start that immediately puts pressure on the peloton. Coastal winds and exposed roads create unpredictable racing conditions, while the scenic route through surf coast communities provides a stunning backdrop.

keyClimbs.name: Opening Climb
keyClimbs.notes: 10km climb at race start, puts weaker riders on the back foot immediately

puncheurs: Riders who can handle the early climb and coastal winds
allRounders: Strong all-rounders who thrive in unpredictable conditions
sprinters: Fast finishers who can survive the early climb

narratives: Mid-week warm-up race before Saturday's WorldTour Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race
First ever women's edition of the Surf Coast Classic - a milestone for equality in Australian cycling
Teams use this as final preparation for the marquee weekend WorldTour event
Coastal winds and exposed roads can split the peloton unexpectedly
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Surf Coast Classic was established as part of the Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race festival week. The women's edition was added to provide parity with the men's race, giving riders an additional competitive opportunity on the Victoria coastline before the weekend's WorldTour action.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The early 10km climb immediately sets the tone - watch for aggressive racing from teams looking to shed pure sprinters. Coastal crosswinds on exposed sections can cause splits. Teams with WorldTour ambitions will use this as a tactical rehearsal for Saturday's bigger prize.

races.name: Surf Coast Classic Men
races.description: ProSeries race from Lorne to Torquay featuring rolling coastal terrain with early climbs - warm-up for Cadel Evans race

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Surf Coast Classic runs 157km from Lorne to Torquay, starting with a 10km climb towards Deans Marsh. From there riders head through Barwon Downs and Forrest before meandering through rural countryside to Modewarre, then back to the coast for the finish. Climbs concentrated in first half, with flatter second half typically resulting in a sprint finish.

keyClimbs.name: Deans Marsh Climb
keyClimbs.notes: First major climb out of Lorne

raceDetails.watchNotes: Crosswinds can split the race on exposed coastal sections. Despite rolling terrain in first half, the race typically comes together for a sprint finish in Torquay. Well-organized leadout trains usually prevail.

broadcast.notes: ProSeries race - check GCN/Eurosport for highlights coverage

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Trofeo Ses Salines takes riders through the southeastern part of Mallorca around the town of Ses Salines, known for its salt flats and flat coastal terrain. This tends to be one of the flatter stages in the Challenge Mallorca series, typically favoring sprinters and fast finishers. The route passes through the agricultural lowlands of southern Mallorca.
narratives: One of the flatter trofeos in the Challenge Mallorca, giving sprinters their best chance of the week
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Ses Salines area in southeastern Mallorca provides flat, open roads that contrast with the mountainous terrain of the northern trofeos. The town takes its name from the historic salt production that has been practiced here since antiquity.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Expect a bunch sprint unless crosswinds from the nearby coast split the peloton. The flat terrain means the sprinter teams should control the race.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Trofeo Serra Tramuntana is the queen stage of the Challenge Mallorca, running 154.3km from Selva to the Santuari de Lluc in the heart of the Serra de Tramuntana mountain range. This is the most mountainous trofeo in the series, taking riders through the dramatic limestone peaks of the UNESCO World Heritage-listed mountain range. The summit finish at the historic Lluc monastery rewards pure climbers.

keyClimbs.name: Santuari de Lluc
keyClimbs.notes: Summit finish at the historic Lluc monastery in the Serra de Tramuntana

narratives: The queen stage of the Challenge Mallorca, the only summit finish in the series
The Serra de Tramuntana provides the most dramatic scenery of the week, with narrow mountain roads winding through limestone peaks
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Serra de Tramuntana, a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2011, is the mountainous backbone of Mallorca running along the northwestern coast. The Santuari de Lluc is the spiritual center of Mallorca, a monastery dating back to the 13th century. The Trofeo Serra Tramuntana takes riders through this iconic landscape.
raceDetails.watchNotes: This is the climber's day in the Challenge Mallorca. The summit finish at Lluc ensures the strongest climbers will be at the front. Watch for attacks on the final ascent.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Trofeo Andratx-Pollenca runs approximately 148km from Andratx on the southwestern coast to Pollenca in the northeast, crossing the Serra de Tramuntana mountain range. The route includes significant climbing through the mountain passes with a hilly finish near the Cap de Formentor peninsula. This is one of the harder trofeos in the series, suited to strong climbers and puncheurs who can handle the accumulated elevation.

keyClimbs.name: Mirador d'es Colomer
keyClimbs.notes: The finish area near Pollenca with views over the Cap de Formentor peninsula

narratives: The penultimate race of the Challenge Mallorca week, crossing the island's dramatic mountain spine
The route from Andratx to Pollenca takes riders through some of Mallorca's most scenic terrain
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Andratx-Pollenca route traverses the full width of the Serra de Tramuntana, linking two of Mallorca's most picturesque towns. Pollenca, at the base of the Cap de Formentor peninsula, has been a draw for artists and cyclists alike for its dramatic coastal mountain scenery.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The crossing of the Serra de Tramuntana wears down the peloton, making the finish around Pollenca selective. Watch for attacks on the final climbs that can shed the pure sprinters.

races.name: Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race (Women)
races.description: Women's Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race 2026 highlights from TNT Sports Cycling. Team tactics and race action.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Mapei Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race – Women is the first one-day race of the Women's WorldTour season. The 141km course winds through the Bellarine Peninsula and Surf Coast, featuring more than 1,500 metres of elevation gain and stunning coastal scenery. The decisive Challambra Crescent climb, topping out 9km from the finish, is the key selection point before the fast finish on the Geelong waterfront.

keyClimbs.name: Challambra Crescent
keyClimbs.notes: The decisive climb - punchy gradient tests sprinters and often creates the winning selection

puncheurs: Strong riders who can accelerate on Challambra and finish fast
sprinters: Fast finishers who can survive the climb and contest the reduced bunch sprint
allRounders: Versatile riders who can handle the undulating terrain and wind

narratives: Opening Women's WorldTour one-day race of the season attracts top global field
Unpredictable race history - no rider has ever won back-to-back editions
Australian home riders have strong record - domestic talent always competitive
Equal prize money and prestige with the men's race demonstrates parity commitment
Final test of the Australian January racing block before teams head to Europe
raceDetails.historicalContext: Named after Australian cycling legend Cadel Evans, the race was first held in 2015 and elevated to Women's WorldTour status in 2020. The race has earned a reputation for unpredictability, often producing surprise winners and close finishes. Australian riders have historically performed well on home soil.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Challambra Crescent climb 9km from the finish is where the race explodes. Watch for attacks on the steeper gradients - the climb isn't long but it's punchy enough to shed pure sprinters. Coastal winds throughout can cause echelons and surprise splits. The fast descent and run-in to the Geelong waterfront favors riders who can recover quickly from the climb effort.

Free-to-air broadcast on Channel 7 in Australia

races.description: The pinnacle of cyclocross - elite women battle for the rainbow jersey in Hulst, Netherlands.

primary.notes: Full race replay available

raceDetails.courseSummary: Hulst's World Championship course features Dutch flat terrain with technical sections and passionate home crowd support.
narratives: Dutch riders competing for home glory
Who can challenge Dutch dominance?
Young talents vs experienced champions
raceDetails.watchNotes: The World Championships is an all-or-nothing race - tactics differ from World Cup rounds.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Grand Prix Cycliste de Marseille La Marseillaise is a 1.1 one-day race of approximately 164km that traditionally opens the French road cycling season. The hilly route starts from Marseille at L'Estaque and finishes in the Luminy district with a short uphill sprint. The course takes riders through the dramatic calanques and limestone massifs east of Marseille, with three main ascents that make the race selective: the Pas de la Couelle, Col de l'Espigoulier, and the Route des Cretes. The Mediterranean climate provides mild February conditions for racing.

keyClimbs.name: Pas de la Couelle
keyClimbs.notes: First major ascent that begins the selection

keyClimbs.name: Col de l'Espigoulier
keyClimbs.notes: The most significant climb of the day, key selection point

keyClimbs.name: Route des Cretes
keyClimbs.notes: Final climb approximately 20km from the finish, often the launchpad for decisive attacks

narratives: The traditional opener of the French cycling season
The hilly parcours through the calanques east of Marseille rewards aggressive racing
The short uphill finish at Luminy can produce dramatic sprint finishes among a reduced group
raceDetails.historicalContext: La Marseillaise has been the traditional curtain-raiser of the French cycling season since its first edition in 1980. The race has grown from a local event to a respected 1.1-category race that attracts ProTeam and occasional WorldTour squads looking for early-season competition. The route through the stunning calanques coastline east of Marseille makes it one of the most scenic early-season races.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Route des Cretes, roughly 20km from the finish, is typically where the race is decided. Watch for attacks here that thin the group before the uphill sprint in Luminy. The Col de l'Espigoulier earlier in the race sets up the final selection.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Pionera Race-SCV is a women's 1.1 one-day race in the Valencia/Alicante region of eastern Spain, running from El Real de Gandia to Cocentaina. The hilly course passes through the scenic inland valleys of the Comunitat Valenciana, with rolling terrain that creates an engaging race for puncheurs and bunch sprinters alike. The race is part of the growing Spanish women's early-season calendar.
narratives: Part of the expanding Spanish women's early-season calendar
The hilly terrain in the Valencia interior creates tactical racing
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Pionera Race is a relatively new addition to the women's UCI calendar in Spain, reflecting the growth of women's professional cycling in the country. The SCV (Sport Cycling Valenciana) organization has developed the race as part of the Comunitat Valenciana's winter cycling program.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The rolling terrain means the race could go to either a reduced group sprint or a solo attacker. Watch for the final hills that could shed the pure sprinters.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Trofeo Palma is the closing race of the Challenge Mallorca series, finishing in the island's capital city. The route typically features circuits in and around Palma with a mix of urban roads and surrounding terrain. As the final trofeo of the week, it often sees tired legs from the accumulated racing and can produce unpredictable results.
narratives: The grand finale of the Challenge Mallorca week in the island's capital
Tired legs after a week of racing can produce unexpected results
raceDetails.historicalContext: Palma de Mallorca, the island's vibrant capital, provides a fitting urban backdrop for the Challenge Mallorca finale. The city's mix of medieval old town, waterfront promenades, and modern boulevards offers varied racing terrain.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The finale can go in many directions - watch for which teams still have fresh riders after the week of racing.

races.name: Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race
races.description: Men's Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race 2026 highlights from TNT Sports Cycling. Sprint finish action.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Cadel Evans Great Ocean Road Race is the second WorldTour event of the season and first major one-day race. Starting and finishing at Geelong waterfront, the 182km route includes over 2,000m of elevation gain through stunning Surf Coast scenery. After coastal roads past Bells Beach and inland farmland hills, the race enters a 21km finishing circuit completed 3.5 times, featuring four ascents of the decisive Challambra Crescent.

keyClimbs.name: Challambra Crescent
keyClimbs.notes: Climbed 4 times on finishing circuit - final ascent 9km from finish

raceDetails.watchNotes: The punchy Challambra climb, topping out 9km from the finish, has a habit of splitting the field before riders head back toward the waterfront for the final dash. Watch for attacks on the steep 11% sections. Race is famously unpredictable - different winning scenarios play out each year.
narratives: WorldTour teams looking for early season form
Sprint or breakaway? The Challambra climb decides
Local Australian riders motivated on home roads

raceDetails.courseSummary: Hulst's World Championship course offers Van der Poel home advantage in his quest for another rainbow jersey.
narratives: Can anyone stop Van der Poel on home soil?
Belgian challenge for Dutch dominance
Young Belgian talents emerging
raceDetails.watchNotes: Van der Poel is the heavy favorite but Worlds always delivers drama. Watch early pacing and late attacks.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The 56th edition of the Etoile de Besseges - Tour du Gard is a 5-stage race covering 640.3km from February 4-8 in the Gard department of southern France. Starting in Bellegarde and finishing in Ales, this early-season stage race combines flat sprinter stages with tougher terrain through the garigue landscape of southern France. The race is one of the first multi-day events of the European season and has traditionally attracted strong fields looking to test their early-season form in the mild Mediterranean climate.

keyClimbs.name: Col de la Baraque
keyClimbs.notes: The longest climb in the race, typically on a hilly stage

narratives: One of the first multi-day races of the European season
The varied terrain suits different rider types across the five stages
Teams use this race as a key form-building block before the spring classics campaign
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Etoile de Besseges has been held since 1971 in the Gard department of southern France. Since 2021 it has been known as the Etoile de Besseges - Tour du Gard. The race is a beloved fixture of the early French calendar, offering a mix of stages through the scenic garigue-covered hills of the Cevennes foothills.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The race typically sees sprinters dominate the flat stages while GC contenders battle on the hillier terrain. The final stage time trial or hilly stage usually decides the overall classification.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The UAE Tour Women is the only Women's WorldTour race in the Middle East, featuring four stages across the United Arab Emirates. The race typically includes three flat sprint stages with potential for echelon action in desert crosswinds, and one decisive summit finish to Jebel Hafeet. The mountain finish, averaging 8-9% gradient over the final 10km, has defined the overall classification in every edition.

keyClimbs.name: Jebel Hafeet

raceDetails.historicalContext: The UAE Tour Women launched in 2023 as the first Women's WorldTour race in the Middle East. Organized by RCS Sport for the Abu Dhabi Sports Council, the race has quickly established itself as a significant early-season test. The Jebel Hafeet climb has become iconic, with the summit finish deciding the overall classification in every edition.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Stages 1, 2, and 4 typically finish in bunch sprints, but desert crosswinds can create echelons and time gaps. Stage 3 to Jebel Hafeet is the queen stage where the GC is decided. Watch for attacks on the steeper sections of the 10km climb. Recovery and positioning through the flat stages is crucial for GC contenders.
raceDetails.gcDynamics: Flat stages → maintain position and bonus seconds → Stage 3 Jebel Hafeet decisive → Final stage survival

The race typically features a mix of terrain including a flat or hilly stage for sprinters, a mountain stage often featuring Mont Ventoux or its approaches, and a final stage suited to puncheurs or time trialists. It serves as an important early-season form indicator for Grand Tour contenders.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Classic Var is a one-day race through the hilly terrain of the Var department in Provence, southern France. The race features a succession of short, punchy climbs through the Mediterranean hills, making it ideal for puncheurs and sprinters who can survive the terrain. It serves as early-season preparation for riders targeting the French spring classics.

puncheurs: Strong finishers who can handle the hilly terrain of Provence
sprinters: Fast finishers who can survive the climbs for a reduced bunch kick

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Classic Var is part of the early-season French racing calendar, taking place in the warm Mediterranean climate of the Var region. It attracts a mix of French continental teams and WorldTour squads using the mild February weather to sharpen early-season form.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The repeated climbs throughout the race wear down pure sprinters, often leaving a reduced group for the finale. Watch for attacks on the final hills - if the group stays together, it becomes a punchy sprint.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Grote Prijs Jean-Pierre Monsere is a 202.5km Belgian one-day race based in Roeselare, West Flanders. Named after the late Belgian world champion who died racing in 1971, the race follows a flat to mildly rolling course through the Flemish countryside. It typically favors fast finishers and sprinters, though the race's length and possible crosswinds add unpredictability.

sprinters: Fast finishers who can handle the long race distance
breakawaySpecialists: Aggressive riders who may exploit crosswinds in the flat Flemish terrain

raceDetails.historicalContext: Named in honor of Jean-Pierre Monsere, the Belgian world champion who tragically died in a racing accident in 1971 at just 22 years old. The race is held in his hometown of Roeselare and serves as a memorial to one of Belgium's most talented riders. It's part of the early Flemish racing calendar.
raceDetails.watchNotes: A flat race through Flanders that typically ends in a sprint, but crosswinds through the exposed Flemish polders can blow the race apart. Watch for echelon formation in windy conditions. The race's length at 202km can tire out pure sprinters.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Midwest Cycling Classic is a 137km women's one-day race held in Belgium. Despite its name suggesting an American event, this is a Belgian race that forms part of the early-season women's calendar. The race features a mix of flat and mildly rolling terrain typical of the Belgian Flemish region.

sprinters: Fast finishers looking for early-season wins
breakawaySpecialists: Aggressive riders who may exploit the race dynamics

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Midwest Cycling Classic is a relatively new addition to the women's Belgian racing calendar. It provides racing opportunities for the women's peloton during the early spring build-up to the major cobbled classics.
raceDetails.watchNotes: A relatively straightforward race profile that typically favors sprinters, though Belgian weather and wind can always add unpredictability. Watch for late attacks if conditions favor the aggressive riders.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour des Alpes-Maritimes et du Var is a 2-stage race through the hills and mountains of the French Riviera hinterland. The race typically features challenging terrain in the Maritime Alps, with a hilly opening stage and a mountain stage with a summit or hilltop finish. The race serves as a stepping stone between early-season warm-weather racing and the spring classics.

keyClimbs.name: Col d'Eze
keyClimbs.description: Classic Riviera climb above Nice, frequently used in races along the Côte d'Azur

keyClimbs.name: Col de Vence
keyClimbs.description: Long climb behind Nice leading to the hilltop village of Vence

climbers: Lightweight climbers who excel on Mediterranean mountain roads
puncheurs: Strong riders who can handle the hilly terrain

narratives: Short stage race format means every climb matters for the overall classification
Teams use this as final preparation before bigger stage races like Paris-Nice or Tirreno-Adriatico
The French Riviera scenery provides a spectacular backdrop
raceDetails.gcDynamics: With only two stages, the GC is typically decided on the mountain stage. Bonus seconds on the first stage can be crucial. Climbers are favored overall.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The race combines the heritage of the former Tour du Haut Var and Tour des Alpes-Maritimes, consolidated into a single two-stage event. It takes place in the same region as Paris-Nice's mountain stages and features similar terrain.
raceDetails.watchNotes: With just 2 stages, the race is compact and aggressive from the start. The mountain stage is where the overall winner will emerge. Watch for attacks on the final climb.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Tour du Rwanda is a prestigious 9-stage race through the 'Land of a Thousand Hills.' The race covers approximately 840km across Rwanda's dramatically hilly terrain, with stages featuring constant climbing and descending through the country's volcanic landscape. Racing at altitudes between 1,400-2,500m above sea level, the thin air and relentless terrain make this one of the most demanding races in Africa.

keyClimbs.name: Wall of Kigali
keyClimbs.description: Iconic finishing climb in the capital city - steep and decisive

keyClimbs.name: Col de Musanze
keyClimbs.description: Long mountain pass through volcanic terrain near the Virunga mountains

gcContenders: African climbing specialists and WorldTour development riders
stageHunters: Breakaway specialists and sprinters for flatter stages

narratives: Tour du Rwanda is Africa's biggest stage race and a key development event for African cycling talent
Altitude and relentless terrain test riders' endurance in ways unlike any European race
Continental and WorldTour development teams battle for supremacy
The passionate Rwandan crowds create an electric atmosphere, especially in Kigali
raceDetails.gcDynamics: The constant climbing means there are few hiding places for GC riders. Mountain stages create significant time gaps, and the altitude factor can surprise riders not acclimatized. The Wall of Kigali often features in the decisive stages.
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1988, the Tour du Rwanda has grown from a regional event to a UCI 2.1 classified race attracting international teams. Rwanda's investment in cycling infrastructure and the race's growing prestige have made it a cornerstone of African cycling development. Many African riders who have gone on to WorldTour careers cut their teeth here.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Every stage features significant climbing due to Rwanda's terrain. Watch for attacks on the longer climbs where altitude takes its toll. The atmosphere is incredible, with huge crowds lining the roads. The race often features aggressive racing from start to finish.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Giro di Sardegna is a multi-stage race across the Italian island of Sardinia. The race features a diverse mix of terrain, from coastal flat stages to challenging mountain stages through Sardinia's rugged interior. The island's windswept roads and punchy climbs create an unpredictable race where both sprinters and climbers can find opportunities.

keyClimbs.name: Sardinian Interior Climbs
keyClimbs.description: Short, steep climbs through the mountainous interior of the island

gcContenders: Stage race specialists and Italian riders using this as Giro d'Italia preparation
stageHunters: Sprinters for flat coastal stages, puncheurs for hilly stages

narratives: Early-season Italian stage race attracts riders preparing for the Giro d'Italia
Sardinia's varied terrain ensures no single rider type dominates
Wind exposure on coastal stages can split the peloton unexpectedly
raceDetails.gcDynamics: The mountain stages decide the GC, but wind on coastal stages can create unexpected splits. The 6-stage format allows for GC battles to develop over multiple days.
raceDetails.historicalContext: The Giro di Sardegna has a long tradition as an early-season Italian stage race. It provides an important racing opportunity for Italian and continental teams while giving WorldTour riders a chance to build form in Mediterranean conditions.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for wind-affected stages along the coast and mountain stages in the interior. The diverse terrain means different types of riders can shine on different days. The race often features aggressive racing from Italian continental teams seeking recognition.

races.name: Omloop Nieuwsblad Women
races.description: Opening Weekend classic through the Flemish Ardennes featuring iconic bergs like the Muur van Geraardsbergen and Bosberg

raceDetails.courseSummary: Omloop Het Nieuwsblad Women is the first major cobbled classic of the European season, traditionally marking the start of 'Opening Weekend' alongside the men's race and Kuurne-Brussel-Kuurne. The 130km route from Ghent to Ninove takes riders through the heart of the Flemish Ardennes, tackling the same iconic bergs (hellingen) and cobbled sectors that define the Tour of Flanders. The decisive Muur van Geraardsbergen and Bosberg climbs come inside the final 15km.

keyClimbs.name: Muur van Geraardsbergen (Kapelmuur)
keyClimbs.notes: The iconic 'Chapel Wall' - steep cobbles through woodland to the chapel at the top. Often race-deciding.

keyClimbs.name: Bosberg
keyClimbs.notes: Final climb with 9km to the finish. Long enough for attackers, close enough for chasers.

keyClimbs.name: Molenberg
keyClimbs.notes: Sharp berg that can trigger early selection

keyClimbs.name: Berendries
keyClimbs.notes: Technical berg before the final showdown

keySectors.name: Paddestraat
keySectors.description: First cobbled sector, sets the rhythm

keySectors.name: Lange Munte
keySectors.description: Long cobbled section through farmland

keySectors.name: Haaghoek
keySectors.description: Rough cobbles that can cause splits before the finale

cobbleSpecialists: Riders who thrive on the rough cobbled bergs
puncheurs: Explosive attackers who can power over the Muur
sprinters: Strong sprinters who can survive the hellingen
allRounders: Versatile riders who can attack and sprint

narratives: Opening Weekend marks the start of the 'real' European racing season
First test on the iconic Flemish climbs before Tour of Flanders
Belgian home riders seek glory on familiar roads
Cold, wet February conditions add to the challenge
Form indicator for the entire Spring Classics campaign
raceDetails.historicalContext: Omloop Het Nieuwsblad has traditionally marked the opening of the Spring Classics since 1945. Named after the Flemish newspaper Het Nieuwsblad, the race takes place in the heart of cycling country, on many of the same cobbles and bergs used in the Tour of Flanders. The women's edition has become a major WorldTour event, often producing surprise winners and dramatic racing.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The Muur van Geraardsbergen is where the race explodes - the steep cobbles shed pure sprinters and force attackers to play their cards. The Bosberg 9km from the finish is the last chance to escape. After the Bosberg, a 9km run-in to Ninove often comes down to a reduced group sprint. Cold conditions and tired legs from winter training can produce unexpected results.

broadcast.notes: Opening Weekend is a major event in Belgium with extensive local TV coverage. Women's race runs alongside the men's event.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Visit South Aegean GP is a one-day race held in the Dodecanese islands of Greece, part of the South Aegean region. The race features coastal roads and hilly terrain on the Greek islands, offering a unique Mediterranean racing experience. The combination of climbs and wind exposure on the island roads creates a challenging course.

puncheurs: Riders who excel on short Mediterranean climbs
allRounders: Versatile riders who can handle both climbs and wind

raceDetails.historicalContext: Part of a series of Greek cycling events in the Dodecanese and South Aegean islands. Greek cycling has been growing in profile with an increasing number of UCI-registered events, and the stunning island settings provide a unique backdrop for racing.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Island racing means wind exposure is a constant factor. The combination of climbs and crosswinds can split the race apart. Watch for attacks on the coastal sections where wind hits the exposed peloton.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Faun Drôme Classic is a French one-day race held in the Drôme department of southeastern France. Starting and finishing in Étoile-sur-Rhône, the 200km route traverses the rolling terrain between the Rhône Valley and the Vercors foothills. Despite its 'flat' classification, the parcours features several short climbs that can break up the peloton before the finale.

sprinters: Fast finishers capable of surviving selective climbs
puncheurs: Riders who can attack on late climbs
allRounders: Strong all-rounders using this as spring season opener

narratives: Early-season test for sprinters ahead of spring classics
French teams looking for home soil victories
Form indicator race for Paris-Nice preparation
raceDetails.historicalContext: Formerly known as the Classic Sud Ardèche, the race was rebranded as the Drôme Classic and has grown in stature as a key early-season race in France. It serves as preparation for both sprinters targeting Milan-San Remo and classics specialists.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Watch for breakaways on the final climbs trying to stay away from the bunch sprint. If the race comes together, expect a bunch sprint where positioning through the final kilometers is crucial.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Kuurne-Brussels-Kuurne is a classic Belgian one-day race held on the day after Omloop Het Nieuwsblad as part of the 'Opening Weekend' of Belgian cycling. The 200km race starts and finishes in Kuurne, West Flanders, traversing through Brussels and tackling multiple bergs and cobbled sectors. The race features the famous hellingen (short steep hills) of Flanders and selective cobbled sections that test spring classics fitness.

keySectors.name: Oude Kwaremont
keySectors.description: Classic Flemish cobbled climb - selective but not decisive at this point

keySectors.name: Koppenberg
keySectors.description: Short but brutal 22% gradient cobbles - often causes splits

cobbleSpecialists: Flanders specialists using this as Tour prep
puncheurs: Riders who can handle the hellingen
sprinters: Fast finishers with cobble survival skills

narratives: Second day of Opening Weekend tests tired legs from Omloop
Flandrien classics specialists fine-tuning form before Tour of Flanders
Weather often plays a major role - wind, rain, and cold add to difficulty
Can sprinters survive the cobbles for a bunch sprint?
raceDetails.historicalContext: First held in 1901, Kuurne-Brussels-Kuurne is one of the oldest races in Belgium. The race gained prominence as the companion event to Omloop Het Nieuwsblad, together forming the unofficial 'Opening Weekend' that kicks off the Belgian spring classics season. Many riders use it as final preparation before the Spring Classics block.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The race can go multiple ways: breakaway success on the bergs, late attacks on the final climbs, or a reduced bunch sprint. Watch for the Koppenberg and Oude Kwaremont to create selection. Tired legs from Omloop the day before often leads to surprises.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Le Samyn des Dames is a 122km women's one-day race in the Hainaut province of Belgium, from Quaregnon to Dour. The race features a circuit around Dour with several short, steep cobbled climbs that test riders' classics credentials. The punchy finale with its hellingen (short cobbled hills) makes this race selective despite its modest distance.

keySectors.name: Cobbled climbs around Dour
keySectors.description: Short, steep cobbled hills that create selection in the finale

cobbleSpecialists: Women who excel on short, steep cobbled climbs
puncheurs: Strong finishers who can attack on the final hills

raceDetails.historicalContext: Le Samyn des Dames is the women's edition of Le Samyn, a Belgian semi-classic. The race is named after José Samyn, a Belgian cyclist who died in a racing accident. It serves as an important early-season Belgian cobbled race for the women's peloton, testing classics form ahead of the major spring campaigns.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The cobbled climbs around Dour are where the race is decided. Watch for attacks on the final laps of the circuit - the repeated efforts on short, steep cobbled hills wear riders down. An uphill finish adds to the selectivity.

raceDetails.courseSummary: The Vuelta a Extremadura Femenina is a women's one-day race in the Extremadura region of western Spain. The race traverses the rolling terrain of this less-visited Spanish region, featuring agricultural landscapes and moderate climbs. It's part of the growing Spanish women's racing calendar.

puncheurs: Riders who handle the rolling terrain of Extremadura
sprinters: Fast finishers if the race comes together

raceDetails.historicalContext: The Vuelta a Extremadura Femenina is part of Spain's expanding women's race calendar. Extremadura, bordered by Portugal, is one of Spain's least densely populated regions and provides scenic racing through its rolling pastoral landscapes.
raceDetails.watchNotes: The rolling terrain means the race could go to either a reduced sprint or a late attack. Spanish early-March weather can be variable, adding an element of unpredictability.

raceDetails.courseSummary: Strade Bianche Women is a 136km one-day classic through the stunning white gravel roads (strade bianche) of Tuscany. Starting from Siena, the race features 8 gravel sectors totaling over 30km of unpaved roads across rolling Tuscan hills before the brutal uphill finish through Siena's medieval streets to Piazza del Campo. The race character demands a combination of gravel handling, climbing ability, and tactical intelligence.

keySectors.name: Sector 1 - Vidritta
keySectors.description: Opening gravel sector, used to shake out the peloton

keySectors.name: Sector 2 - Bagnaia
keySectors.description: Rolling gravel with some gradient changes

keySectors.name: Sector 3 - Radi
keySectors.description: Long gravel sector that begins to thin the peloton

keySectors.name: Sector 5 - San Martino in Grania
keySectors.description: The longest sector - decisive terrain where attacks often stick

keySectors.name: Sector 8 - Le Tolfe
keySectors.description: Final gravel sector with steep gradients, last chance for gravel specialists

keySectors.name: Via Santa Caterina
keySectors.description: The iconic final climb through Siena's narrow streets - steep ramps of up to 16% on worn brick surface

classicSpecialists: Riders with gravel and classics pedigree who can handle the terrain
puncheurs: Strong finishers who can power up the Via Santa Caterina
climbers: Lighter riders who can attack on the gravel hills

narratives: The women's Strade Bianche has rapidly become one of the most prestigious one-day races on the WWT calendar
The unpredictable Tuscan gravel roads reward boldness - late attackers on sectors 5-8 often decide the race
The Piazza del Campo finish is among the most spectacular in cycling - narrow medieval streets into the shell-shaped square
Weather can transform the race - rain turns gravel roads into mud, making descents treacherous and attacks more rewarding
raceDetails.historicalContext: Strade Bianche Women was first held in 2015 and quickly became a marquee event on the women's calendar. The race showcases the unique white clay gravel roads of the Crete Senesi region of Tuscany. It shares the same iconic Piazza del Campo finish with the men's event and has produced some of the most dramatic finishes in women's cycling.
raceDetails.watchNotes: Key moments come on sectors 5-8 in the final 50km where the gravel gets more demanding and the hills steeper. The race often comes down to a small group or solo attack on the final gravel sectors before the Via Santa Caterina climb. Disable autoplay and avoid sidebar recommendations.

races.name: Paris-Nice

stages.name: Stage 1: Achères - Carrières-sous-Poissy
stages.description: Punchy opener with two ascents of the steep Côte de Chanteloup-les-Vignes in the final 30km, favoring puncheurs over pure sprinters.

stageDetails.courseSummary: Rolling terrain with four category three climbs in the first 100km. The decisive feature is the double ascent of Côte de Chanteloup-les-Vignes in the final 30km. Steep gradients make this a challenging opener for the sprinters.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Chanteloup-les-Vignes
keyClimbs.notes: Ascended twice in final 30km

stages.name: Stage 2: Épône - Montargis
stages.description: Sprinters' best opportunity with three early categorized climbs and potential crosswinds through exposed Gâtinais sections.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The sprinters' stage of Paris-Nice 2026. Three categorized climbs are confined to the first 120km, providing breakaway opportunities. The exposed Gâtinais plains could create crosswind splits before a likely bunch sprint in Montargis.

stages.name: Stage 3: Cosne-Cours-sur-Loire - Pouilly-sur-Loire (TTT)
stages.description: Team time trial across rolling Nièvre terrain with intermediate time check at Saint-Laurent-L'Abbaye (14km).

stageDetails.courseSummary: A 23.5km team time trial over rolling terrain in the Nièvre region. Intermediate time check at Saint-Laurent-L'Abbaye at 14km. The road rises to Saint-Andelain before descending toward Pouilly-sur-Loire. Individual GC times taken at the finish.

stages.name: Stage 4: Bourges - Uchon
stages.description: Predominantly flat until the final 40km, finishing with the demanding category one Signal d'Uchon (8km at 4.5%, pitches to 16%).

stageDetails.courseSummary: A long day that stays flat for 155km before the road tilts upward for the decisive finale. The category two Côte de la Croix des Cerisiers leads into the category one Signal d'Uchon summit finish, a significant GC battleground.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de la Croix des Cerisiers
Signal d'Uchon
keyClimbs.notes: Summit finish, decisive GC climb

stages.name: Stage 5: Cormoranche-sur-Saône - Colombier-le-Vieux
stages.description: The longest stage at 206.3km with three steep climbs after the intermediate sprint, including the brutal Côte de Saint-Jean-de-Muzols (2.2km at 11%).

stageDetails.courseSummary: The race's longest stage packs its punch in the second half. After the intermediate sprint, three steep climbs will test the peloton. The Côte de Saint-Jean-de-Muzols (2.2km at 11%) and Côte de Saint-Barthélémy-le-Plain (3.2km at 7.6%) are particularly demanding, with the road climbing to the finish line.

stages.name: Stage 6: Barbentane - Apt
stages.description: Four categorized climbs with the final Côte de Saignon (4km at 5%) cresting 4.5km from the finish, rewarding skilled descenders into Apt.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A Provençal hilly stage with four categorized climbs concentrated in the second half. The first 100km are nearly flat before the Col de l'Aire Deï Masco (7.1km at 4.4%) and the final Côte de Saignon (4km at 5%) summit 4.5km from the finish. The sharp descent into Apt rewards skilled descenders.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Saignon
keyClimbs.notes: Final climb before descent to finish

stages.name: Stage 7: Nice - Auron
stages.description: Summit finish at the Auron ski station with a final ascent of 7.3km at 7.2%, the penultimate stage GC showdown.

stageDetails.courseSummary: A mountain stage finishing at the Auron ski station. Climbing begins from the outset with the Côte de Carros and Côte de Bouyon, followed by an 80km transitional section with minimal elevation. The final ascent to Auron (7.3km at 7.2%) is the major GC battleground of the race.

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Carros
keyClimbs.notes: Early climb from Nice

keyClimbs.name: Côte de Bouyon
keyClimbs.notes: Second early climb

keyClimbs.name: Auron
keyClimbs.notes: Summit finish at ski station

stages.name: Stage 8: Nice - Nice
stages.description: Final stage with three categorized climbs including the punishing Côte du Linguador (3.3km at 8.8%, max 14%), the last chance to shake up GC before Nice.

stageDetails.courseSummary: The final stage loops through the hills around Nice with nearly 2,800m of climbing. Three categorized climbs define the day: Col de la Porte (7km at 7.2%), Côte de Châteauneuf-Villevieille (6.6km at 6.6%), and the fearsome Côte du Linguador (3.3km at 8.8%, max 14%). Less than 20km remains after the final summit, allowing continued attacks before crowning the overall winner.

keyClimbs.name: Côte du Linguador
keyClimbs.notes: Final categorized climb, less than 20km from finish
